title: The Silmarillion
author: J. R. R. Tolkien
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "The Silmarillion" book by J. R. R. Tolkien?
Options:
A. After that final ship, no more came from the West to Númenor, and Avallónë was shrouded in cloud. Gimilkhâd passed away two years before reaching his second century, unusually young for one of Elros' line even in its fading years. Yet his death brought no calm to the King, for Pharazôn his son had grown into a man even more desiring of riches and authority than his father. Pharazôn had journeyed frequently overseas, commanding in the wars by which the Númenóreans sought to spread their sway over the Men of the coasts of Middle-earth. Thus he had earned high renown as a leader on both land and sea.  
B. But no ship came ever again from the West to Nmenor, and Avalln was veiled in cloud. Now Gimilkhd died two years before his two hundredth year (which was accounted an early death for one of Elros' line even in its waning), but this brought no peace to the King. For Pharazn son of Gimilkhd had become a man yet more restless and eager for wealth and power than his father. He had fared often abroad, as a leader in the wars that the Nmenreans made then in the coastlands of Middle-earth, seeking to extend their dominion over Men; and thus he had won great renown as a captain both by land and by sea.
C. That was the last ship that ever came from the West to Númenor, and Avallónë was covered in clouds after that. Gimilkhâd died at the age of 198, which was considered an early passing for one of Elros' descendants, even as their lifespans waned. But his death did not bring peace to the King, because his son Pharazôn had become a man who lusted for wealth and power even more than his father did. Pharazôn had often traveled abroad as a commander in the wars where the Númenóreans attempted to expand their rule over the Men living along the coasts of Middle-earth. Through this he had won great fame as a leader of both land and naval forces.
D. But no more ships came from the West to Númenor after that, and Avallónë was covered in clouds. Gimilkhâd died when he was 198 years old, which was considered an early death for one of Elros' descendants even though their lifespans were decreasing. But his death did not bring peace to the King, because his son Pharazôn, who was even more eager for wealth and power than his father, had become a man. Pharazôn had often gone abroad as a leader in the wars where the Númenóreans tried to extend their rule over other Men in the coastlands of Middle-earth. Thus he had won great fame as a captain on both land and sea.
Answer:

Gimilkhâd passed away two years before reaching his second century, unusually young for one of Elros' line even in its fading years. Pharazôn had journeyed frequently overseas, commanding in the wars by which the Númenóreans sought to spread their sway over the Men of the coasts of Middle-earth. Thus he had earned high renown as a leader on both land and sea.
